scene: A lively mix of young professionals, students, and a growing nomad crowd, with warm tapatío hospitality and an emerging LGBTQ-friendly nightlife. Friend-making is easy if you speak some Spanish; dating and going out are vibrant and affordable.

name: Sayulita
description: A bohemian Pacific surf town on the Riviera Nayarit, famous for its colorful streets, consistent waves, and laid-back nomad scene. Young surfers and remote workers flock here for the tropical beach lifestyle, yoga studios, and tight-knit international community. It's small, walkable, and unapologetically boho.
vibe_summary: Surfboards, papel picado, and barefoot beach-town bohemia on the Pacific.
tax_notes: Foreign income generally untaxed for nomads on a temporary resident visa, ideal for remote-working surfers.
lowdown: Sayulita is a colorful Pacific surf town that's gone from sleepy to buzzy, packed with beginner-friendly waves, taco stands, and a boho nomad crowd. It's walkable and fun but small, and popularity has pushed prices up while straining water, parking, and crowds in high season. Internet can be patchy and it's more vacation-vibe than long-haul practical. Ideal for surfers and creatives who want a tropical, social base for a season or two.
scene: A young, international mix of surfers, nomads, and yoga types layered over a Mexican village; intensely social and easy to meet people in a single week. Nightlife is beachy and bohemian, dating scene transient.

name: Lake Atitlan
description: A stunning volcanic lake ringed by Mayan villages, a magnet for spiritual seekers, wellness travelers, and budget nomads. Young people come for the dramatic scenery, cheap living, yoga and permaculture community, and slow off-grid lifestyle. It's remote and rustic but deeply beautiful.
vibe_summary: Volcanoes mirrored in a sacred lake, drawing yogis, healers, and dreamers.
tax_notes: Guatemala taxes only locally-sourced income, so foreign remote earnings are effectively untaxed for nomads.
lowdown: Lake Atitlán is jaw-droppingly beautiful — a volcano-ringed lake dotted with distinct villages, each with its own character. It's extremely cheap, deeply spiritual, and a magnet for wellness seekers and long-term wanderers. Infrastructure is rough: boat-only access between towns, unreliable power and internet, and basic healthcare. Best for budget-minded, adaptable people chasing nature, yoga, and a slow off-grid-ish life over convenience.
scene: A patchwork of indigenous Maya communities and a transient wellness/hippie expat crowd concentrated in San Marcos and San Pedro; warm but transient. Easy to find a healing circle, harder to find a stable long-term community.

name: Roatan
description: A Caribbean island off Honduras famous for world-class, affordable diving on the Mesoamerican Reef. Young divers, expats, and nomads come for the turquoise water, low costs, and easygoing island pace. Infrastructure is basic but the underwater life is spectacular.
vibe_summary: Turquoise reefs, dive boats, and barefoot island living on the cheap.
tax_notes: Honduras taxes territorially in practice, so foreign income earned by nomads is generally not taxed locally.
lowdown: Roatán is a Caribbean island built around world-class, affordable diving and a warm laid-back pace. Costs are low and the reef is spectacular, drawing divers, retirees, and a small expat scene. The flip side is island reality: limited healthcare, occasional power and water issues, and safety that requires the usual common sense. Suits divers and beach lovers who want tropical living on a budget and don't mind island constraints.
scene: A small, friendly mix of dive instructors, expats, retirees, and locals, centered on West End and West Bay; easy to know everyone fast. Nightlife is casual beach bars; dating pool tiny and transient.

name: Nosara
description: An upscale jungle-meets-surf town on Costa Rica's Nicoya Peninsula, a global capital of yoga and wellness tourism. Young nomads and wellness seekers come for consistent surf, pristine beaches, and a thriving international community. It's pricier than most of Central America but built for the conscious lifestyle.
vibe_summary: Howler monkeys, sunrise surf, and yoga mats unrolled in the jungle.
tax_notes: Costa Rica taxes only domestic-source income and offers a nomad visa exempting foreign earnings from local tax.
lowdown: Nosara is a wellness-and-surf haven on Costa Rica's Nicoya Peninsula, known for consistent waves, jungle-meets-beach setting, and a strong yoga culture. It's gone upscale and pricey for the region, with bumpy dirt roads, a remote location, and a long drive from the airport. The pura vida pace and nature are the draw; convenience and value are not. Ideal for surfers and wellness devotees who'll pay for a healthy, off-the-grid-ish tropical life.
scene: An affluent, health-focused international community of surfers, yogis, and families alongside locals; welcoming if you're into the wellness lifestyle. Nightlife is mellow and early; social life revolves around the beach, yoga, and sunset.

name: Washington DC
description: The US capital, a polished, diverse city of politics, policy, and global institutions with free world-class museums. Young professionals come for careers in government, law, nonprofits, and tech, plus a walkable core and strong transit. It's expensive but cultured and international.
vibe_summary: Monuments, think-tanks, and ambitious young professionals in a green, diverse capital.
tax_notes: Federal tax plus a fairly high DC local income tax makes the overall burden steep for residents.
lowdown: Washington DC is a walkable, monument-studded city dense with museums, policy careers, and an international crowd. It's expensive but pays well, with great public transit and a serious food scene that's grown fast. The work culture can feel buttoned-up and transient, and humid summers are sticky. Best for the ambitious in government, law, policy, or nonprofits who want a cultured, walkable East Coast base.
scene: Educated, career-driven, and international, heavy on policy wonks and young professionals; friendly but networking-flavored and people cycle in and out with administrations. Nightlife and dating are active, if a touch resume-conscious.

name: Atlanta
description: A sprawling Southern metropolis that's become a hub for tech, film, music, and Black entrepreneurship. Young professionals come for strong career growth, relatively affordable big-city living, lush green suburbs, and a powerhouse nightlife and music scene. Traffic and sprawl are the downsides.
vibe_summary: Hip-hop heart and a booming new-South business engine wrapped in tree canopy.
tax_notes: Federal tax plus moderate Georgia state income tax; the world's busiest airport makes it a connectivity king.
lowdown: Atlanta is the cultural and economic capital of the South — a Black cultural powerhouse with booming film, music, and startup scenes and far more affordable housing than the coasts. It's sprawling and car-dependent with notorious traffic, and summers are hot and humid. The food and nightlife are excellent and the energy is rising fast. Suits ambitious creatives and professionals who want opportunity and culture without coastal prices.
scene: Young, diverse, and stylish, with a thriving Black professional and creative class and serious nightlife; sociable and welcoming once you find your pocket. Dating scene is big and active, though the sprawl means everything's a drive.

name: Calgary
description: A clean, prosperous prairie city at the gateway to the Canadian Rockies, with Banff and world-class skiing an hour away. Young professionals come for strong energy and tech salaries, no provincial sales tax, and unmatched mountain access. Winters are long and bitterly cold.
vibe_summary: Big-sky prairie boomtown with the Rockies and Banff right out the back door.
tax_notes: High Canadian federal and Alberta provincial income tax, though Alberta has no provincial sales tax.
lowdown: Calgary sits at the doorstep of the Rockies, offering big-mountain access, a clean modern downtown, and no provincial sales tax. The economy leans on energy, which makes it boom-and-bust, and winters are long and brutally cold with sudden chinook swings. It's more affordable than Vancouver or Toronto and easy to get around. Ideal for outdoor lovers who want world-class skiing and hiking with a stable, spacious city base.
scene: Friendly, down-to-earth, and outdoorsy, with a young professional crowd and a strong work-hard-play-hard streak; making friends is easier here than in Canada's bigger cities. Nightlife is solid but the real social life is on the trails and slopes.

name: Brisbane
description: A sunny, laid-back river city that's grown into a real hub for young professionals and remote workers without Sydney's price tag. Warm weather year-round, an easy outdoorsy lifestyle, and a fast-growing tech and startup scene make it a magnet for under-35s.
vibe_summary: Subtropical ease meets ambitious new-city energy along a lazy bending river.
tax_notes: Australia taxes residents on worldwide income with high marginal rates, though working-holiday and skilled visas are common entry points.
lowdown: Brisbane is sunny, relaxed, and increasingly cosmopolitan, a more affordable and easygoing alternative to Sydney and Melbourne. The subtropical climate means warm winters but humid, stormy summers, and it's a bit quieter culturally than its bigger siblings. It's growing fast ahead of the 2032 Olympics, with a friendly outdoor lifestyle and good coffee. Suits people who want Australian sunshine and a laid-back city without the top-tier price tag.
scene: Welcoming, sporty, and unpretentious, with a young professional and student crowd; friendlier and less cliquey than Sydney. Nightlife is solid in pockets like Fortitude Valley; dating and social life skew casual and outdoorsy.

name: Byron Bay
description: An iconic beach town that's become a global byword for wellness, surf and a slower, barefoot-luxe lifestyle. Young creatives, remote workers and yoga-and-smoothie types flock here for the beaches, hinterland and easy-going community.
vibe_summary: Surfboards, sunrise yoga and bohemian wealth wrapped around a perfect bay.
tax_notes: Standard Australian resident tax rules apply, with high marginal rates on worldwide income for long-stayers.
lowdown: Byron Bay is Australia's wellness-and-surf icon: gorgeous beaches, hinterland forests, and a barefoot, holistic ethos. Its fame has made it expensive and crowded, with housing scarce and prices rivaling the big cities. The lifestyle is genuinely beautiful but the town is small and can feel like a curated influencer set in peak season. Best for affluent surfers and wellness lovers who'll pay a premium for the coastal-bohemian dream.
scene: An affluent mix of surfers, wellness entrepreneurs, creatives, and celebrities over a small local base; social and open but pricey to keep up with. Nightlife is mellow and early; the vibe is sunrise yoga over late nights.

name: Cairns
description: The tropical gateway to the Great Barrier Reef and Daintree rainforest, drawing backpackers, divers and adventure-seekers from around the world. Warm year-round and packed with reef trips, waterfalls and a steady stream of young travelers.
vibe_summary: Reef-and-rainforest adventure base where the tropics meet a backpacker buzz.
tax_notes: Australian resident taxation applies; popular with working-holiday visa holders in tourism and hospitality.
lowdown: Cairns is the tropical gateway to the Great Barrier Reef and Daintree rainforest, a small city built around adventure and diving. It's affordable for Australia and laid-back, but the wet-season humidity is intense, stinger season limits ocean swimming, and it's remote from the rest of the country. Job options outside tourism are thin. Ideal for divers and nature lovers who want reef-and-rainforest access over urban amenities.
scene: A small, transient mix of backpackers, dive workers, and locals; easy to meet travelers, harder to build a lasting community. Nightlife is backpacker-driven and casual; dating pool small and rotating.

name: Gold Coast
description: A glitzy strip of high-rises, surf beaches and theme parks that's also quietly become a remote-work and startup hotspot. Young people come for the endless beach lifestyle, nightlife and a warm, active outdoor culture.
vibe_summary: Neon-lit surf city where beach days roll straight into big nights out.
tax_notes: Residents face high Australian marginal rates on worldwide income; entry usually via skilled or working-holiday visas.
lowdown: The Gold Coast is Australia's beach-and-party playground: 35 miles of surf beaches, high-rises, theme parks, and a year-round holiday buzz. It's sunnier and a bit cheaper than Sydney, with a booming surf and fitness culture, but the glitzy Surfers Paradise strip can feel touristy and shallow. Beyond the towers there's a genuine laid-back beach-suburb life. Suits surfers and sun-lovers who want a fit, social, beach-first lifestyle.
scene: Young, tanned, and fitness-obsessed, with a big party-and-surf crowd plus growing families and remote workers; very social and easy to meet people at the beach or gym. Nightlife is loud and lively in Surfers; dating scene active.

name: Melbourne
description: Australia's cultural capital, famous for laneway cafes, world-class food, live music and a dense arts and startup scene. Endlessly walkable and creative, it's a perennial favorite for young professionals and remote workers despite changeable weather.
vibe_summary: Coffee, vinyl and graffitied laneways in a moody, culture-soaked metropolis.
tax_notes: High Australian resident tax rates on worldwide income, mitigated only for short-stay working-holiday visa earners.
lowdown: Melbourne is Australia's culture capital — laneway cafés, world-class food, live music, sport, and a creative, European-flavored buzz. It's walkable with great trams and a strong arts scene, but the weather is famously fickle (four seasons in a day) and grayer than the rest of the country. Costs are high though slightly under Sydney. Best for foodies, creatives, and culture lovers who'll trade reliable sun for the best city life in Australia.
scene: Cool, creative, and diverse, with a deep arts, coffee, and sports culture; sociable and welcoming, with the country's best nightlife and dating scene. Easy to find your tribe across countless subcultures.

name: Noosa
description: An upscale Sunshine Coast beach town known for its national park, gentle surf and refined, health-focused lifestyle. It draws affluent young remote workers and wellness types who want beaches and bushwalks without the high-rises.
vibe_summary: Polished coastal calm where national-park trails spill onto golden beaches.
tax_notes: Standard Australian resident tax rules apply, with high marginal rates on worldwide income.
lowdown: Noosa is an upscale, eco-conscious beach town on the Sunshine Coast, with a national park headland, gentle surf, and a refined low-rise charm (no high-rises by design). It's beautiful, safe, and family-friendly, but expensive and quiet, skewing older and wealthier. The pace is slow and the nightlife minimal. Ideal for affluent beach-and-nature lovers who want a polished, relaxed coastal life over buzz.
scene: An affluent, health-minded mix of families, retirees, and wellness types; pleasant and friendly but not a young-singles hub. Social life centers on the beach, river, hiking, and brunch; nightlife is sleepy.

name: Perth
description: One of the world's most isolated big cities, with sun-drenched beaches, a booming resources economy and an easy, outdoorsy lifestyle. Young professionals enjoy high wages, white-sand coastlines and a relaxed pace far from the eastern crowds.
vibe_summary: Endless Indian Ocean sunsets and big skies at the edge of the continent.
tax_notes: Australian resident taxation on worldwide income applies, with high marginal rates offset by strong mining-driven wages.
lowdown: Perth is one of the world's most isolated cities, and that buys it gorgeous empty beaches, sunny Mediterranean weather, and a relaxed, spacious lifestyle. The isolation is also the catch: it's a long flight from anywhere, culturally quieter, and reliant on a mining-driven economy. Costs are moderate by Australian standards. Best for sun-and-beach lovers who value space and calm over big-city culture and connectivity.
scene: Friendly, outdoorsy, and unhurried, with a mix of locals, miners, and transplants; warm but a bit insular given the isolation. Nightlife is decent in Northbridge; social life leans heavily on beach and barbecue.

name: Sydney
description: Australia's flagship global city, wrapped around one of the world's great harbours and famous beaches like Bondi. It offers a glamorous outdoor lifestyle, the country's biggest job and startup market, and a vibrant, diverse social scene for young people.
vibe_summary: Harbour glitter, Bondi mornings and a fast, sun-soaked metropolitan pulse.
tax_notes: High Australian resident tax rates on worldwide income make Sydney costly for long-stayers despite strong salaries.
lowdown: Sydney is Australia's showpiece — a stunning harbor city with iconic beaches, a great climate, and a polished, outdoorsy lifestyle. It's also one of the world's most expensive cities, with brutal housing costs and a long commute reality for many. The natural setting and beach culture are world-class, but you pay dearly for it and the social scene can feel status-conscious. Best for high earners who want beach-city beauty and will pay top dollar to live it.
scene: Beautiful, beachy, and a touch image-aware, with a big mix of locals and global transplants; sociable but the cost and sprawl can make deeper friendships work. Nightlife is good (if regulation-dented) and the dating scene is large and active.

name: Fiji
description: A tropical archipelago of over 300 islands famed for turquoise lagoons, world-class diving and warm, welcoming island culture. Increasingly popular with remote workers and surfers chasing a slow, sun-soaked Pacific lifestyle.
vibe_summary: Palm-fringed lagoons and unhurried island warmth in the heart of the Pacific.
tax_notes: Fiji has moderate income taxes and offers long-stay options, though only locally sourced income is generally taxed for many expats.
lowdown: Fiji is the postcard South Pacific: warm turquoise water, friendly locals, and a genuinely relaxed island pace. It's more affordable than Tahiti and welcoming to long-stayers, but island life means limited healthcare, slower internet, occasional cyclones, and getting between islands takes planning. Modern amenities concentrate in Nadi and Suva. Best for beach-and-surf lovers who want warm, friendly tropical living and can roll with island infrastructure.
scene: Famously warm, hospitable locals plus a small expat and resort-worker mix; easy to feel welcome, though a deep long-term peer community takes effort. Nightlife is low-key and resort-centered; social life is beachy and communal.

name: Bora Bora
description: A postcard-perfect lagoon island ringed by overwater bungalows and coral reefs, synonymous with luxury Pacific escape. It draws honeymooners and well-off remote workers seeking turquoise water and total tranquility, though it's remote and very pricey.
vibe_summary: Overwater bungalows on an impossibly blue lagoon beneath a jade volcanic peak.
tax_notes: French Polynesia levies no personal income tax, but the cost of living and import duties are extremely high.
lowdown: Bora Bora is the dream-honeymoon island — a turquoise lagoon ringed by a volcanic peak and overwater bungalows. It's breathtaking but eye-wateringly expensive, tiny, and built almost entirely around luxury tourism, so everyday living costs and imports are brutal. There's little here beyond the lagoon: limited services, healthcare, and almost no job market. Best only for the wealthy who want a remote slice of paradise and don't need a city's conveniences.
scene: A small local Polynesian community plus a rotating luxury-resort crowd; warm but not a place to build a broad social or dating life. Nightlife is essentially resort dinners and sunset cocktails.

name: Tahiti
description: The lush, mountainous main island of French Polynesia and its cultural and economic heart, with black-sand beaches and legendary surf at Teahupo'o. It blends Polynesian culture with French infrastructure, drawing surfers and adventurous remote workers.
vibe_summary: Jagged green peaks, black-sand surf breaks and laid-back French-Polynesian soul.
tax_notes: No personal income tax in French Polynesia, though high import-driven costs offset the tax advantage.
lowdown: Tahiti is the urban heart of French Polynesia, with the islands' main airport, hospital, and a real (if small) city in Papeete. It offers black-sand beaches, world-class surf at Teahupo'o, and lush volcanic scenery, but it's expensive thanks to heavy import reliance, and Papeete itself is more workaday than postcard. It's the practical base for island life with French infrastructure. Best for surfers and Francophiles who want Polynesian living with actual amenities.
scene: A mix of Polynesian locals, French expats, and a surf crowd; French helps a lot socially, and the community is tight-knit. Nightlife is modest and Papeete-centered; social life leans family, surf, and lagoon.

name: Auckland
description: New Zealand's largest and most diverse city, set between two harbours with beaches, islands and volcanic cones at its doorstep. It's the country's economic and startup hub, offering young professionals a multicultural city with easy access to nature.
vibe_summary: Sail-dotted harbours and volcanic green hills around a relaxed Pacific-Rim city.
tax_notes: New Zealand has no general capital gains tax but taxes worldwide income for residents at moderate-to-high rates.
lowdown: Auckland is New Zealand's biggest, most multicultural city, set between two harbors with beaches, islands, and volcanoes at hand. It offers a high quality of life and great food, but housing is very expensive, the city sprawls, and the weather is mild but often wet and windy. It's the country's economic engine but can feel low-key versus global hubs. Best for people who want a safe, scenic, English-speaking base with easy nature access.
scene: Diverse and easygoing, with strong Pacific, Asian, and Māori influences; friendly but, like much of NZ, deep friendships can take time as locals have settled circles. Nightlife is decent in pockets; social life is outdoorsy and harbor-centered.
not_for: anyone wanting a major global hub

name: Christchurch
description: A South Island city reborn after earthquakes into an innovative, green and increasingly creative hub, and the gateway to the Southern Alps and Canterbury Plains. Young people get an affordable-by-NZ-standards city with skiing, hiking and surf all within reach.
vibe_summary: Rebuilt garden city where rebuilt laneways open onto the wild Southern Alps.
tax_notes: No general capital gains tax in New Zealand, with moderate income tax on residents' worldwide earnings.
lowdown: Christchurch is the rebuilt 'Garden City' of the South Island, a flat, easy, increasingly modern base for accessing the Southern Alps and Pacific coast. Since the 2011 quakes it has reinvented itself with fresh architecture and a creative streak, and it's more affordable than Auckland. Winters are cold and it can feel quiet and spread out. Best for outdoor lovers who want an affordable, English-speaking gateway to South Island adventure.
scene: Friendly, practical, and outdoorsy, with a young rebuilding energy and a growing creative scene; welcoming but socially low-key. Nightlife is modest; weekends revolve around skiing, hiking, and the coast.

name: Queenstown
description: The adventure capital of the world, cradled by mountains and a glacial lake, famous for bungee jumping, skiing and jaw-dropping scenery. It draws a young, transient crowd of seasonal workers, adrenaline-seekers and remote workers living for the outdoors.
vibe_summary: Snow-capped peaks plunging into a turquoise lake fueling nonstop adventure.
tax_notes: New Zealand's lack of capital gains tax appeals to investors, though resident income is taxed at moderate rates.
lowdown: Queenstown is the adventure capital of the world — bungee, ski fields, hiking, and a jaw-dropping alpine-lake setting in every direction. It's spectacular and buzzy year-round but small, tourist-driven, and very expensive, with a chronic housing shortage and seasonal-work economy. The lifestyle is incredible if adventure is your life; the trade-off is cost and a transient population. Best for outdoor obsessives who'll organize their whole life around mountains and lakes.
scene: A young, international, adventure-mad crowd of seasonal workers, instructors, and travelers; easy to meet people fast, but turnover is high and roots are shallow. Nightlife is lively for its size; dating scene transient and fun.

name: Wellington
description: New Zealand's compact, hilly capital, known for its creative film and tech industries, cafe culture and craft beer scene. Walkable and culturally rich, it punches above its weight for young professionals despite famously windy weather.
vibe_summary: Windswept harbour capital buzzing with coffee, craft beer and creative energy.
tax_notes: No general capital gains tax, but residents pay moderate-to-high income tax on worldwide earnings.
lowdown: Wellington is New Zealand's compact, creative capital — a walkable harbor city packed with cafés, craft beer, film industry (Weta), and the best arts scene in the country. It's cultured and friendly with great food, but famously windy, and housing is pricey for its size. The compact scale is a charm and a limit. Best for creatives and culture lovers who want a small, walkable, artsy city over big-city scale or reliable weather.
scene: Smart, artsy, and sociable, with a strong film, music, and café culture; the small scale makes it easy to plug into creative circles. Nightlife and dating punch above the city's size; community feels close-knit.

name: Adelaide
description: A relaxed, affordable coastal city surrounded by world-famous wine regions and Mediterranean-style climate. Young professionals enjoy a walkable center, festival culture and an easygoing lifestyle at a fraction of Sydney or Melbourne costs.
vibe_summary: Wine country at the edges of an unhurried, festival-loving coastal city.
tax_notes: Standard Australian resident taxation on worldwide income, with high marginal rates.
lowdown: Adelaide is Australia's underrated, easygoing wine-and-food city, with a Mediterranean climate, gorgeous nearby vineyards, and a relaxed, affordable pace. It's clean, walkable, and far cheaper than the big east-coast cities, with a strong festival and culinary culture. The trade-off is a quieter economy and a reputation for being a bit sleepy. Best for foodies and value-seekers who want Australian quality of life without Sydney or Melbourne prices.
scene: Friendly, laid-back, and unpretentious, with a tight food-and-wine community and a growing young creative crowd; easier to settle into than the bigger cities. Nightlife is modest but the festival season brings real buzz; social life centers on food, wine, and the beach.

name: Hobart
description: Tasmania's compact harbour capital, beneath Mount Wellington, famed for wilderness, the MONA art museum and a thriving food and arts scene. It draws young creatives and nature lovers wanting an affordable, slow-paced base near pristine outdoors.
vibe_summary: Misty mountain backdrop, edgy art and wild Tasmanian nature on the doorstep.
tax_notes: Australian resident taxation applies, with high marginal rates on worldwide income.
lowdown: Hobart is small, slow, and ringed by mountains and water, with a creative streak punched well above its weight by MONA and a serious food-and-wine culture. Life here is quiet and outdoorsy: weekend bushwalks, Salamanca markets, cold clear air, early closing times. It suits people who want nature on the doorstep and a tight community, but the isolation is real and winters are genuinely cold and dark by Australian standards. Housing has gotten tight and pricey for what's still a small city.
scene: A mix of fifth-generation Tasmanians, mainland transplants chasing a calmer life, and an arts crowd orbiting MONA; friendly but small, so you make friends through clubs and repeat run-ins rather than a big nightlife churn.

name: Raglan
description: A famous black-sand surf town on New Zealand's North Island, home to one of the world's longest left-hand point breaks. It's a small, bohemian community of surfers, artists and eco-minded remote workers living a laid-back coastal life.
vibe_summary: Legendary left-hand point break and a barefoot, arty surf-town soul.
tax_notes: New Zealand has no general capital gains tax, with moderate income tax on residents' worldwide income.
lowdown: Raglan is a laid-back surf town on New Zealand's west coast, famous for one of the best left-hand point breaks in the world and a barefoot, eco-minded pace. Days revolve around tides, a single walkable main street of cafes and craft shops, and a strong sense of community. It's tiny, so jobs are scarce and you'll drive to Hamilton for anything serious, and the weather is windy and wet for stretches. Best for people who genuinely want to opt out of city life.
scene: A blend of Maori locals, surfers, hippies and remote workers; small and welcoming if you show up regularly, with a low-key scene built around the surf, the pub, and house gatherings rather than nightlife.

name: Nadi
description: Fiji's main tourism gateway on Viti Levu's west coast, home to the international airport and a launchpad to the Mamanuca and Yasawa islands. Warm, affordable by island standards and full of beaches and reefs, it's a practical base for Pacific island life.
vibe_summary: Sun-baked island gateway where reef trips and resort life begin.
tax_notes: Fiji levies moderate income taxes and offers accessible long-stay visas, with favorable treatment of foreign-sourced income for many expats.
lowdown: Nadi is Fiji's gateway town, where most visitors land before scattering to the islands, so it's more functional than beautiful — markets, the airport, tour operators, and access to the Mamanuca and Yasawa boats. Living here is warm, slow, and friendly, with cheap fresh produce and easy reef access, but it's humid year-round, infrastructure is patchy, and the town itself lacks charm. It suits people who want a tropical base near surf and diving and don't mind a rough-around-the-edges hub. Healthcare and amenities are limited.
scene: Mostly local Fijian and Indo-Fijian community with a rotating cast of tourists and a small expat/resort-worker crowd; genuinely warm people, but a transient feel and a thin scene for long-term socializing.

name: Bariloche
description: An alpine lakeside town in the Argentine Andes famous for skiing, chocolate and dramatic Patagonian scenery. Young travelers come for ski seasons, trekking and a relaxed mountain-town pace with easy access to wilderness.
vibe_summary: Swiss-style chalets and turquoise lakes ringed by snow-capped peaks, with après-ski energy in winter.
tax_notes: Argentina has high marginal rates and complex currency controls, though nomads often live on undeclared foreign income.
lowdown: Bariloche is an alpine lake town in Argentine Patagonia, all snow-capped peaks, deep blue lakes, chocolate shops and ski slopes. Life is outdoorsy and seasonal: skiing and hot chocolate in winter, hiking, kayaking and trout fishing in summer. It's affordable by global standards and beautiful, but it's remote, the economy swings with tourism and inflation, and winters are long. Great for an outdoor-obsessed lifestyle if you don't need a big job market.
scene: A friendly mix of Argentines who relocated for the lifestyle, outdoor guides, and a growing remote-work crowd; social life centers on the mountains, asados, and a modest but warm local scene.
not_for: anyone needing a deep job market

name: Buenos Aires
description: A sprawling, European-flavored capital known for tango, late-night culture, world-class steak and an explosive arts scene. Cheap living amid currency swings draws a large remote-work and creative crowd to its leafy, cafe-dense barrios.
vibe_summary: Belle Époque boulevards meet all-night milongas and a bohemian, intellectual energy that never quite sleeps.
tax_notes: High personal rates and currency controls make formal tax onerous, but expats benefit from favorable parallel exchange rates.
lowdown: Buenos Aires is a sprawling, romantic, slightly chaotic metropolis with European architecture, world-class food, and a nightlife that genuinely starts at midnight. It's culturally rich and, thanks to a weak peso, can be remarkably cheap for those earning in dollars, though inflation makes daily prices a moving target and bureaucracy is exhausting. The city rewards people who love art, cafes, late dinners, and an intense urban energy. Downsides are economic instability, some petty crime, and patchy infrastructure.
scene: A huge, social, night-owl population of porteños plus a big and growing digital-nomad and expat crowd; easy to meet people through milongas, language exchanges and a buzzing bar scene, with famously late, social dating culture.

name: Mendoza
description: Argentina's wine capital at the foot of the Andes, surrounded by Malbec vineyards and Aconcagua's snowy peaks. A sunny, laid-back base for wine tourism, rafting and mountaineering.
vibe_summary: Tree-lined avenues, leisurely vineyard lunches and big mountain horizons in every direction.
tax_notes: Same high-rate Argentine regime applies, partly offset for foreigners by the favorable blue-dollar exchange.
lowdown: Mendoza is Argentina's wine capital, a sunny, tree-lined city at the foot of the Andes surrounded by vineyards and snow-capped peaks. Life is relaxed and outdoorsy — wine country weekends, rafting, skiing a couple of hours away, long lunches — with a dry, sunny climate most of the year. It's affordable and the pace is gentle, but it's a mid-size city without the cultural depth of Buenos Aires, and the economy is tourism- and agriculture-dependent. Ideal for wine, sun, and mountains over big-city buzz.
scene: A laid-back local crowd plus wine-industry workers and a trickle of expats and nomads; social and welcoming, with life revolving around bodegas, asados and outdoor trips rather than a dense nightlife.
not_for: anyone needing a major job market

name: Florianópolis
description: An island city famed for 40-plus beaches, surf breaks and a fast-growing tech scene that's earned it the nickname Brazil's Silicon Island. A magnet for surfers, startup founders and remote workers seeking beach-and-laptop living.
vibe_summary: Sun-bleached surf towns and lagoon parties blend with a buzzy entrepreneurial energy.
tax_notes: Brazil taxes residents on worldwide income at up to 27.5%, but a digital-nomad visa lets foreigners stay on foreign earnings.
lowdown: Florianópolis is an island city in southern Brazil with 40-plus beaches, a growing tech scene, and a quality of life that draws Brazilians and nomads alike. The north is touristy and lively, the south wild and surfy, the center a real working city. It's safer and more orderly than much of Brazil, with great surf and lagoon life, but it gets crowded and pricey in summer, and you really need a car. A strong pick for beach-plus-startup living.
scene: A mix of southern Brazilians, domestic transplants, surfers, and a sizable digital-nomad and startup crowd; sociable and easygoing, with beach days, lagoon bars and a fun, flirty nightlife in season.

name: Rio de Janeiro
description: An iconic coastal metropolis of mountains, beaches and Carnival, where Copacabana and Ipanema meet samba and street life. Intensely social and outdoorsy, it draws those chasing beach culture, music and dramatic urban nature.
vibe_summary: Sugarloaf silhouettes, beach-volleyball afternoons and samba that spills into the streets.
tax_notes: Worldwide income is taxed up to 27.5% for residents, though nomads on the foreign-income visa avoid local liability.
lowdown: Rio is pure spectacle — mountains plunging into the sea, beach culture as a way of life, samba, and an energy few cities match. Living here means Sugarloaf views, beach mornings, and a deeply social outdoor culture, balanced against real concerns about safety, inequality, and inconsistent infrastructure. It suits people who can roll with a bit of chaos and prioritize beauty, beach, and nightlife. Pick your neighborhood carefully and street-smarts are non-negotiable.
scene: Famously warm, social, beach-living cariocas plus expats and nomads clustered in the Zona Sul; easy to meet people on the sand and at parties, with a vibrant, flirtatious nightlife and dating scene.

name: São Paulo
description: Latin America's largest city and economic engine, with an unrivaled food scene, world-class nightlife and the continent's deepest startup ecosystem. A dense, cosmopolitan grind that rewards ambitious professionals and creatives.
vibe_summary: Endless skyline, legendary restaurants and a relentless 24-hour cultural pulse.
tax_notes: Residents face up to 27.5% on worldwide income; the digital-nomad visa shields foreign-sourced earnings.
lowdown: São Paulo is Latin America's economic engine — a vast, gritty, endless metropolis with the continent's best restaurants, deepest job and startup market, and a serious cultural and nightlife scene. It's not pretty and the traffic and sprawl are punishing, but for ambition, food, art, and music it's unmatched in the region. It suits career-driven people and culture obsessives who want a real big city over postcard views. Safety varies by area and the scale can overwhelm.
scene: A massive, diverse, hardworking and hard-partying population with a big professional, creative, and expat crowd; easy to find your niche given the size, with an elite food scene and legendary nightlife.

name: Santiago
description: A modern, well-organized capital wedged between the Andes and the Pacific, with ski slopes and vineyards within an hour. Its stable economy, clean infrastructure and 'Chilecon Valley' startup scene attract professionals and remote workers.
vibe_summary: Snow-capped peaks over a glassy skyline, with weekend skiing, surf and wine all within reach.
tax_notes: Chile taxes residents progressively up to 40%, but newcomers get a three-year exemption on foreign-source income.
lowdown: Santiago is a modern, well-run capital framed by the Andes, with reliable infrastructure, a strong economy, and skiing and wine country both within an hour. It's the most orderly and developed big city in the region — clean metro, good services, a real startup scene — but it can feel buttoned-up and socially harder to crack, and winter smog settles in the basin. It suits people who want Latin American life with first-world functionality. Cost of living is higher than its neighbors.
scene: A reserved, professional local population plus a solid expat and entrepreneur crowd; warm once you're in but slower to make friends than elsewhere in Latin America, with a decent if low-key nightlife.

name: Valparaíso
description: A bohemian Pacific port city of steep hills, colorful funiculars and sprawling street art, beloved by artists and free spirits. Its faded grandeur and creative energy make it a quirky, affordable coastal alternative to Santiago.
vibe_summary: Rainbow houses tumbling down hillsides, clattering funiculars and murals around every corner.
tax_notes: Same Chilean regime as Santiago, with a three-year foreign-income exemption easing the burden for arriving expats.
lowdown: Valparaíso is a faded, bohemian port city of steep hills, colorful houses, funiculars, and street art tumbling down to the Pacific. It's romantic and creative, cheaper and looser than Santiago, with a strong arts and poetry heritage. The flip side is genuine grit — dilapidation, petty crime, and uneven services — so it rewards people who love character over polish. Great for artists and writers who want atmosphere and sea views on a budget.
scene: An arty, student, and bohemian crowd mixed with old port families and a thin expat scene; creative and friendly, with bar life in the hills, though the dating and nightlife pool is smaller than the capital.

name: Bogotá
description: Colombia's high-altitude capital, a cool, green Andean metropolis with a thriving arts scene, growing tech sector and abundant cheap living. Its spring-like climate and energetic nightlife draw remote workers seeking an affordable big-city base.
vibe_summary: Misty mountain backdrop over brick neighborhoods, weekend bike-filled streets and a buzzing café and club scene.
tax_notes: Colombia taxes residents on worldwide income up to 39%, but its digital-nomad visa lets foreigners stay without local tax on foreign earnings.
lowdown: Bogotá is Colombia's high-altitude capital, a sprawling, cool-weather city with a serious cultural and culinary scene, a growing tech sector, and prices that go a long way for dollar earners. It's grey and rainy more than people expect, traffic is brutal, and safety requires street smarts, but the food, museums, and music are excellent and the energy is real. It suits people who want an affordable, culturally deep big city and don't need beaches or sun. The altitude takes adjusting to.
scene: A large, fast-moving local population plus a fast-growing digital-nomad and expat crowd centered in the north; sociable and easy to meet people through the bar, salsa, and nomad scenes, with lively nightlife.

name: Cartagena
description: A sultry Caribbean walled city of colonial mansions, bougainvillea-draped balconies and turquoise waters. A romantic, party-friendly coastal escape popular for getaways, weddings and beachfront remote work.
vibe_summary: Pastel colonial streets, horse-drawn carriages and rooftop sunsets over the Caribbean.
tax_notes: Standard Colombian rates up to 39% on residents, with the digital-nomad visa exempting foreign-sourced income.
lowdown: Cartagena is a postcard Caribbean colonial city — walled old town, balconies dripping with flowers, warm sea, and a heavy tourist trade. Life here is hot, humid, and sociable, with beach trips, rooftop bars, and a romantic historic core, but the constant tourism, heat, and price gouging in the center wear on residents. It suits people who want tropical beauty and party energy over depth or efficiency. Outside the pretty zones it's a hot working city.
scene: A mix of costeño locals, a party-oriented tourist flow, and a small expat crowd; easygoing and flirtatious with a strong rooftop-and-beach nightlife, though long-term friendships take more effort amid the churn.

name: Medellín
description: The 'City of Eternal Spring,' a reinvented Andean valley city with perfect year-round weather, innovative transit and a famously friendly social scene. One of the world's top digital-nomad hubs, with affordable living and a huge expat community in El Poblado and Laureles.
vibe_summary: Spring-warm valley nights, hillside cable cars and a relentlessly social, salsa-soaked energy.
tax_notes: Colombia taxes residents up to 39%, but the popular digital-nomad visa keeps foreign income out of the local tax net.
lowdown: Medellín sits in a green Andean valley with spring-like weather all year, which is the whole draw — that plus a low cost of living has made it a nomad magnet. The transformation story is real, with cable cars, good food, and a buzzing Poblado scene, but that popularity has driven up prices and tension in expat zones, and safety still demands awareness. It suits remote workers and social types who want comfort, climate, and nightlife on a budget. Look beyond Poblado to find the real city.
scene: A famously warm paisa population plus one of Latin America's densest digital-nomad and expat scenes; extremely easy to meet people, with intense nightlife and a dating scene that's a major draw and a frequent point of friction.

name: Santa Marta
description: A laid-back Caribbean gateway where the Sierra Nevada mountains meet the sea, near Tayrona National Park's jungle beaches. A budget-friendly base for backpackers, divers and nature lovers chasing both coast and rainforest.
vibe_summary: Palm-lined bays, jungle-backed beaches and an unhurried backpacker-coast rhythm.
tax_notes: Colombian residents are taxed up to 39% on worldwide income, while nomads on the digital-nomad visa avoid local tax.
lowdown: Santa Marta is a hot Caribbean coastal city that doubles as the gateway to Tayrona National Park and the Sierra Nevada mountains, so you get beach, jungle, and trekking from one base. It's cheaper and less polished than Cartagena, more of a working town with rough edges, but the access to wild nature and diving is exceptional. It suits adventurers and budget travelers who prioritize the outdoors over urban comforts. The heat is relentless and infrastructure is basic.
scene: A mostly local coastal population with a backpacker and small expat flow passing through; friendly and informal, with a modest nightlife and a transient social scene built around nearby adventures.

name: Quito
description: Ecuador's high-altitude capital, home to one of the best-preserved colonial old towns in the Americas, surrounded by volcanoes. Spring-like weather, low costs and the US dollar economy make it an easy, affordable Andean base.
vibe_summary: Baroque church domes against snow-capped volcanoes, with cool, clear equatorial mountain air.
tax_notes: Ecuador uses the US dollar and taxes residents up to 35%, but foreign-source income is generally exempt for nomads.
lowdown: Quito is a high-altitude Andean capital with a stunning UNESCO colonial old town, dramatic mountain surroundings, and spring-like weather year-round. It's very affordable, walkable in parts, and central for exploring the Andes and Amazon, but the altitude is no joke, traffic and pollution are real, and safety has slipped in recent years. It suits budget-minded people who love history, mountains, and mild weather over beaches or buzz. A bit sleepy after dark.
scene: A reserved local population plus a modest expat and volunteer crowd; pleasant but slower to build a social life than Colombia or Argentina, with a quieter nightlife concentrated in a few districts.

name: Cusco
description: The ancient Inca capital high in the Andes, gateway to Machu Picchu and a hub of indigenous culture and adventure tourism. Cobblestone streets, ruins and trekking draw a steady stream of travelers and a small nomad crowd.
vibe_summary: Inca stonework beneath colonial arches, llamas in the plaza and thin, golden mountain light.
tax_notes: Peru taxes residents up to 30% but exempts foreign-source income for the first years of residency, easing things for nomads.
lowdown: Cusco is the ancient Inca capital high in the Peruvian Andes, a gorgeous stone-and-tile city that's the launchpad for Machu Picchu and the Sacred Valley. Living here is cheap and visually spectacular, steeped in history and surrounded by epic trekking, but the 11,000-foot altitude is demanding, nights are cold, and the center is heavily geared to tourists. It suits adventurers and history lovers who can handle the altitude and a tourism-driven economy. Not a place for beach or modern-city comforts.
scene: A mix of Quechua and mestizo locals, a steady backpacker stream, and a small expat and guide community; warm and informal, with friendships forming around trekking and a lively but tourist-leaning nightlife.

name: Lima
description: Peru's sprawling coastal capital and one of the world's great food cities, with cliff-top districts overlooking the Pacific. Surf, ceviche and an emerging startup scene anchor an affordable big-city base in districts like Miraflores and Barranco.
vibe_summary: Foggy ocean cliffs, surfers below and a gastronomic obsession around every corner.
tax_notes: Peruvian residents pay up to 30%, but foreign-source income is exempt for the first years, benefiting incoming nomads.
lowdown: Lima is a vast coastal capital perched on cliffs above the Pacific, home to one of the world's great food scenes and a surprisingly good surf-in-the-city lifestyle in the right districts. It's affordable and culturally rich, but the city is grey and overcast for much of the year, traffic is chaotic, and safety varies sharply by neighborhood. It suits foodies and surfers who want an affordable big city and can tolerate the gloom and sprawl. Stick to the coastal districts and it shines.
scene: A large, diverse local population with a modest expat and nomad presence clustered in Miraflores and Barranco; sociable in those zones with a strong food-and-bar culture, though the broader city is harder to crack.

name: Montevideo
description: A calm, safe and progressive capital on the River Plate, with a long beachfront promenade and an easygoing, European pace. Uruguay's stability, strong institutions and tax incentives make it a quietly appealing base for remote workers.
vibe_summary: Sea-breeze rambla strolls, mate gourds in hand and a relaxed, civic-minded calm.
tax_notes: Uruguay offers new residents a multi-year tax holiday on foreign income, making it unusually favorable for nomads.
lowdown: Montevideo is a calm, safe, low-key capital with a long riverfront rambla, a relaxed pace, and a reputation as one of the most stable and progressive countries in Latin America. It trades excitement for security and quality of life: clean, walkable, socially liberal, with good beaches an hour away. The tradeoff is that it's quiet, pricier than its neighbors, and can feel sleepy if you crave intensity. It suits people who prioritize safety, stability, and an easy coastal life.
scene: A friendly, easygoing, progressive local population with a small expat crowd; mate-sharing and beach-walking define the social rhythm, with a relaxed, LGBTQ-friendly scene and a low-key nightlife.

name: Cuenca
description: A graceful colonial city in the southern Ecuadorian Andes, with cobblestone streets, river walks and a famously mild climate. Its low costs, dollar economy and sizeable retiree-and-expat community make it an easy, tranquil landing spot.
vibe_summary: Red-tile roofs and blue domes along willow-lined rivers, with cool, eternal-spring air.
tax_notes: Ecuador's dollarized system taxes residents up to 35%, but foreign-source income is generally exempt for incoming expats.
lowdown: Cuenca is a graceful colonial city in the southern Ecuadorian Andes, with cobblestone streets, red-domed cathedrals, four rivers, and a famously mild climate. It's become a top retirement and expat haven for good reason: very low cost of living, walkability, good healthcare for the price, and a calm, cultured pace. The flip side is that it's quiet, at high altitude, and rainy, and the large retiree-expat presence isn't for everyone. It suits people seeking affordable, peaceful, culturally rich living.
scene: A polite, traditional local population alongside one of Latin America's biggest North American retiree-expat communities; easy to plug into the expat circuit, though the nightlife and younger dating scene are limited.

name: Montanita
description: A tiny, hard-partying surf village on Ecuador's Pacific coast, legendary among backpackers for its waves and nonstop nightlife. Rustic, cheap and bohemian, it's a magnet for surfers and free-spirited travelers.
vibe_summary: Bamboo bars, cocktail alleys and reliable point-break waves under a permanent beach-party haze.
tax_notes: Ecuador's dollar economy exempts most foreign income for nomads, with resident rates reaching up to 35%.
lowdown: Montañita is a small, ramshackle surf-and-party village on Ecuador's coast, equal parts good waves and all-night cocktail-street revelry. It's cheap, warm, and easy to fall into a beach-bum rhythm, but it's also dusty, basic, and very party-centric, which gets old fast for anyone not there to surf and drink. It suits young surfers and travelers wanting sun, waves, and nightlife on the cheap. Not a place for comfort, quiet, or long-term depth.
scene: A transient mix of backpackers, surfers, and a small bohemian expat crowd; intensely social and party-driven, with an easy hookup-and-hostel nightlife but little in the way of stable long-term community.

name: Cali
description: Colombia's salsa capital, a warm, energetic valley city with a passionate dance culture and famously sociable nightlife. Affordable and intense, it draws those chasing music, dance and tropical heat.
vibe_summary: Sweltering nights, spinning salsa clubs and an irrepressibly social, rhythm-driven energy.
tax_notes: Colombian residents pay up to 39% on worldwide income, while the digital-nomad visa keeps foreign earnings untaxed locally.
lowdown: Cali is the salsa capital of the world, a hot, energetic valley city in southwestern Colombia where dance is genuinely woven into daily life. It's cheaper and less touristy than Medellín, with warm people, great weather, and an unmatched music scene, but safety is a bigger concern and infrastructure is rougher. It suits people who want authentic Colombian culture, heat, and dancing over polish or expat comforts. You'll want decent Spanish and street smarts.
scene: A warm, dance-obsessed caleño population with a small but growing expat and nomad presence; extremely social once you're in the salsa scene, with vibrant nightlife and a flirtatious dating culture, though it's less expat-paved than Medellín.

name: La Paz
description: The world's highest administrative capital, a dramatic Andean city sprawled across a canyon with cable-car transit and vibrant indigenous markets. Raw, cheap and adventurous, it's a gateway to extreme landscapes and a budget traveler's hub.
vibe_summary: Brick city climbing canyon walls under snowy peaks, with cable cars gliding over bustling cholita markets.
tax_notes: Bolivia levies a flat 13% personal tax and generally taxes only Bolivian-source income, leaving foreign earnings untouched.
lowdown: La Paz is a jaw-dropping city sprawled across a canyon at over 11,000 feet, with cable cars gliding between neighborhoods and the Andes towering above. It's one of the cheapest and most culturally distinct capitals in South America, intensely indigenous and chaotic in the best way, but the altitude is severe, infrastructure is basic, and it's a hard place to live comfortably long-term. It suits adventurous, budget-driven people who want something genuinely different. Not for those needing modern comforts or oxygen.
scene: A predominantly Aymara and mestizo local population with a thin backpacker and expat presence; warm but harder to integrate without Spanish, with a modest nightlife and a small long-term foreign community.
not_for: anyone wanting a big expat scene

name: Arequipa
description: Peru's elegant 'White City,' built of pale volcanic stone beneath three towering volcanoes, with a sunny climate and refined colonial core. Affordable and scenic, it's a gateway to canyon trekking and a quieter alternative to Cusco.
vibe_summary: Sun-bright volcanic-stone facades framed by perfect snow-capped cones and crisp highland air.
tax_notes: Peru taxes residents up to 30% but exempts foreign-source income in early residency years, helping incoming nomads.
lowdown: Arequipa is Peru's elegant 'White City,' built from pale volcanic stone and ringed by three volcanoes, with a sunny dry climate and a more relaxed pace than Lima or Cusco. It's affordable, beautiful, and lower-altitude than Cusco, with great access to the Colca Canyon and good food, but it's a mid-size provincial city without a deep job market or big international scene. It suits people who want colonial beauty, sun, and mountains on a budget. Quieter and more conservative than the capital.
scene: A traditional, proud local population with a small expat and student presence; pleasant and safe-feeling but slower socially, with a modest nightlife and friendships built through Spanish and routine.

name: Cordoba
description: Argentina's lively second city and a major university town, with a youthful energy, colonial heritage and nearby sierras for weekend escapes. Its student population, low costs and growing tech sector make it an affordable, social base.
vibe_summary: Historic Jesuit blocks meet a buzzing student nightlife, with green sierra hills just out of town.
tax_notes: Argentina's high rates and currency controls apply, partially offset for foreigners by the favorable parallel exchange rate.
lowdown: Córdoba is Argentina's second city, a youthful university town with colonial architecture, a strong music and nightlife culture, and the green sierras just outside town for weekend escapes. It's affordable, lively, and less overwhelming than Buenos Aires, with a big student energy and a genuine cultural scene, though the same Argentine economic instability applies and the summer heat is intense. It suits younger people and culture lovers who want city life and nature access on a budget. Less international than the capital.
scene: A large student and young-professional population that makes for an easy, social, party-friendly scene; meeting people is straightforward through university life and nightlife, with a smaller expat presence than Buenos Aires.

name: Asuncion
description: Paraguay's low-key riverside capital, one of the cheapest and least-touristed cities in South America, with an easygoing pace and territorial tax regime. Its ultra-low taxes and simple residency draw budget-minded entrepreneurs and nomads.
vibe_summary: Hot, sleepy riverside streets, faded grandeur and an unhurried, off-the-radar calm.
tax_notes: Paraguay's territorial system taxes only local income at a flat 10%, leaving foreign earnings tax-free for residents.
lowdown: Asunción is one of South America's most overlooked and affordable capitals, a hot, low-rise riverside city with a laid-back pace and very low cost of living. It's drawing budget-minded nomads and residency-seekers for its cheap living and easy tax setup, but it's sleepy, hot, infrastructure is basic, and there's little tourism polish or cultural buzz. It suits people who want maximum affordability and a quiet base over excitement or scenery. English is rare and Spanish (plus Guaraní) is essential.
scene: A friendly, traditional local population with a small but growing expat and residency-driven nomad crowd; warm and welcoming but a thin social scene, with limited nightlife and a community you build slowly.
